Name: spectral_stability_bounds_for_low_latency_inference
Title: Spectral Stability Bounds for Low-Latency Inference in Deep Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that spectral stability bounds can be used to derive enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference in deep neural networks, enabling the development of more efficient and reliable machine learning models.
Related Work: Recent works such as SNIP, AMP-ViT, and EMQ have focused on optimizing the efficiency of deep neural networks, but have not explicitly addressed the issue of spectral stability bounds for low-latency inference. Our proposal builds upon these works by introducing a novel framework that leverages spectral stability bounds to optimize the inference process.
Abstract: Deep neural networks have achieved state-of-the-art performance in various applications, but their inference process can be computationally expensive and prone to latency issues. To address this challenge, we propose a novel framework that leverages spectral stability bounds to optimize the inference process. Our approach uses PAC-Bayes frameworks linked to spectral stability bounds to derive enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through experiments on various benchmark datasets, showing significant improvements in inference efficiency while maintaining accuracy.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Evaluate the effectiveness of our approach on benchmark datasets such as ImageNet and CIFAR-10.
Experiment 2: Compare our approach with state-of-the-art methods such as SNIP and AMP-ViT.
Experiment 3: Investigate the impact of spectral stability bounds on the inference process using ablation studies.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed approach may not be applicable to all types of deep neural networks.
The derivation of spectral stability bounds may require significant computational resources.
The approach may not be robust to adversarial attacks or outliers in the data.
